c9a05975.content:
Chief Complaint:
Mr. Lewis, a 65‑year‑old male, presents with a 2‑week history of increasing fatigue, vague right upper quadrant (RUQ) discomfort, decreased appetite, and an unintentional 10 lb weight loss over the past three months. He reports yellowing of the skin and progressive abdominal bloating.

History of Present Illness:
The patient reports progressively worsening fatigue and RUQ ache. No radiation, no precipitating factors noted. He denies fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel habits. He reports drinking 2–3 alcoholic beverages per day over many years and has a 40‑pack‑year smoking history. The patient has noted worsening jaundice over the past week.

Past Medical History:
• Chronic hepatitis B (HBsAg positive)
• Chronic hepatitis C (HCV antibody positive, HCV RNA 2.1 × 10^6 IU/mL)
• Cirrhosis of the liver (diagnosed 5 years ago)
• Metabolic syndrome, obesity (BMI 32)
• Type 2 diabetes mellitus
• Hypertension
• Hyperlipidemia

Past Surgical History:
None.

Medications:
None at present.

Allergies:
No known drug allergies.

Social History:
• 65‑year‑old male, retired farmer.
• Current alcohol use: 2–3 drinks per day.
• Former smoker, 40 pack‑years, quit 5 years ago.
• No illicit drug use.

Review of Systems:
Constitutional: Fatigue, weight loss, decreased appetite.
HEENT: Jaundice noted.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations.
Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
Gastrointestinal: RUQ discomfort, abdominal bloating, no nausea or vomiting.
Genitourinary: No dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: No recent injuries.
Skin: Scleral icterus, no rash.
Neurologic: No focal deficits.

Physical Examination:
General: 65‑year‑old male, appears chronically ill, in no acute distress.
Vital Signs:
• BP: 155/88 mmHg
• HR: 90 bpm
• Temp: 98.2 °F
• RR: 16/min
• SpO2: 98% RA
• Weight: 190 lb
• BMI: 32

HEENT:
Scleral icterus present.

Cardiovascular:
Regular rhythm, no murmurs.

Pulmonary:
Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen:
Mild ascites noted on palpation. Soft, non‑tender liver edge a centimeter below the costal margin. No shifting dullness. No organomegaly beyond mild hepatomegaly.

Extremities:
No edema.

Labs (POC and inpatient order set):
• CBC: Hgb 12.5 g/dL (anemic), normal WBC and platelets.
• CMP: Bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, AST 70 U/L, ALT 55 U/L, Albumin 2.9 g/dL.
• Hepatitis serology: HBsAg +, HCV Ab +, HCV RNA 2.1 × 10^6 IU/mL.
• Ferritin 480 ng/mL.
• Transferrin saturation 60%.
• Alpha‑fetoprotein: 45 ng/mL.
• POC INR: 1.4.

Assessment:
1. Decompensated cirrhosis secondary to chronic HBV/HCV infection. Liver synthetic function impaired (low albumin, elevated INR). Clinical signs: jaundice, ascites, mild encephalopathy (fatigue).
2. Suspected hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – AFP mildly elevated at 45 ng/mL and weight loss. Needs prompt imaging.
3. Possible iron overload (high ferritin and transferrin saturation) secondary to chronic liver disease.
4. Metabolic syndrome with associated obesity, diabetes, hypertension – contributors to hepatic decompensation.

Differential Diagnosis:
- Hepatic decompensation (ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy)
- HCC
- Alcoholic hepatitis flare
- Iron overload/hemochromatosis
- Bacterial peritonitis (ruled out by lack of fever, tachycardia)
- Post‑hepatic biliary obstruction (unlikely given labs)

Plan:
1. Immediate imaging: Contrast‑enhanced abdominal CT scan or hepatic MRI within 1–2 weeks to evaluate for focal lesions consistent with HCC.
2. Initiate antiviral therapy for HBV (consider tenofovir alafenamide or tenofovir disoproxil fumarate). Discuss initiation of direct‑acting antiviral (DAA) regimen for HCV (e.g., sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) after imaging.
3. Monitor coagulation: Repeat INR in 1 week; begin vitamin K 10 mg PO if INR worsens.
4. Manage ascites: Low‑salt diet (<2 g sodium/day), start spironolactone 40 mg PO daily with furosemide 20 mg PO daily; titrate over 1–2 weeks to reduce ascites.
5. Hepatic encephalopathy: Enforce strict sodium restriction, consider lactulose 25 mL PO Q6h if neuro signs worsen.
6. Counsel on alcohol cessation using the ABCD approach, offer referral to Alcohol Use Disorder treatment program.
7. Diabetes: Continue current regimen; review with endocrinology for adjustment due to hepatic impairment.
8. Weight management: Referral to dietitian, discuss gradual caloric deficit, physical activity plan tailored for cirrhosis.
9. Smoking: Continue encouragement to remain smoke‑free; offer nicotine replacement if desired.
10. Vaccinations: update hepatitis A vaccine if not immune; consider pneumococcal and influenza vaccines.

Medications (current and started):
• None currently prescribed.

Follow‑up Recommendations:
• Return in 2 weeks for imaging review and repeat labs (CBC, CMP, INR).
• Urgent referral to gastroenterology/hepatology for ultrasound/CT evaluation and possible biopsy.
• If ascites worsening despite diuretics, consider peritoneal tap for evaluation of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
• Schedule a multidisciplinary visit with dietitian and endocrinology within 4 weeks.
• Reassess alcohol cessation status and provide resources in subsequent visit.
• Monitor for signs of variceal bleeding (hematemesis, melena) and counsel patient to seek emergent care if occurs.

The patient was educated on the seriousness of the findings, the importance of antiviral therapy adherence, and the need for close follow‑up. He verbalized understanding and expressed motivation to quit alcohol and improve lifestyle factors.
follow_up_recommendations: Return in 2 weeks for imaging review and repeat labs
Urgent referral to GI/hepatology for abdominal imaging
Monitor ascites and titrate diuretics
Counsel on alcohol cessation and refer to treatment program
Referral to dietitian and endocrinology for weight and glucose management
Advise patient to seek emergent care if signs of variceal bleeding develop

c9a05975.content:
Chief Complaint: Evaluation of liver lesion identified on prior ultrasound.

History of Present Illness: Mr. Lewis, a 65‑year‑old male with a history of chronic hepatitis B and C, compensated cirrhosis, metabolic syndrome, obesity, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, presents for a follow‑up ultrasound performed to evaluate a newly noted focal hepatic lesion.  He reports a slight worsening of right upper quadrant (RUQ) discomfort over the past week and mild dizziness.  He denies any new jaundice, weight loss, fever, or significant changes in stool/color.  His viral hepatitis is controlled on tenofovir alafenamide and sofosbuvir/velpatasvir.  An AFP level drawn recently has risen to 45 ng/mL.

Physical Examination: (No formal exam noted during this encounter.  Vital signs recorded as described.)

Vital Signs: BP 150/84 mmHg; HR 88 bpm; Temp 98.4 °F; RR 16 breaths/min; weight 190 lb; BMI 32; SpO2 98% on room air.

Diagnostic Imaging: Contrast‑enhanced abdominal ultrasound revealed a 3.5 cm hypoechoic lesion in segment 6 surrounded by heterogeneous parenchyma suggestive of early cirrhosis.  Moderate hepatic steatosis was noted, with no obvious portal hypertension or ascites.

Assessment & Differential Diagnosis:
1. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – most likely given underlying cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis B/C, elevated AFP, and focal hypoechoic lesion.
2. Benign hepatic neoplasm (e.g., focal nodular hyperplasia, hepatic adenoma) – less likely but considered.
3. Metastatic disease – possible given the patient's comorbidities.
4. Hepatic cyst or abscess – unlikely based on imaging characteristics.
5. Hemangioma – typically hypervascular, not fitting the hypoechoic appearance.

Plan:
- Refer patient to tertiary center for contrast‑enhanced multidetector CT of the abdomen with liver protocol to better characterize the lesion’s vascularity and borders.
- Order liver function tests (ALT, AST, bilirubin, albumin, INR) and complete metabolic panel in the next 1‑2 weeks to monitor liver synthetic function and metabolic status.
- Continue current antiviral therapy (tenofovir alafenamide, sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) and diuretic regimen (spironolactone 40 mg PO daily, furosemide 20 mg PO daily).
- Reinforce dietary modification (low‑salt, low‑fat, weight‑loss plan) and exercise as tolerated.
- Counsel on alcohol avoidance and review vaccination status (hep A/B, influenza).
- If CT findings are suspicious for malignancy, discuss possible percutaneous core‑needle biopsy and/or referral to hepatobiliary surgery.
- Schedule a follow‑up appointment in 4 weeks after CT results are returned to review imaging and adjust management.

The patient was informed of the plan and verbalized understanding.
symptoms_reported: Slight worsening right upper quadrant discomfort
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule contrast‑enhanced abdominal CT within 2 weeks
Return visit 4 weeks after CT to review findings
Continue antiviral and diuretic therapy
Maintain weight loss and blood sugar control
Monitor liver function tests and AFP every 4–6 weeks
Refer to hepatology or surgical oncology if lesion suggests malignancy

c9a05975.content:
Chief Complaint: Evaluation after imaging – biopsy and portal hypertension assessment.

History of Present Illness:
65‑year‑old male with chronic HBV and HCV infection, cirrhosis, metabolic syndrome, obesity, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Recent contrast‑enhanced CT abdomen showed a 3.5‑cm hepatic lesion in segment 6 with arterial phase hyperenhancement and portal washout, highly suggestive of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Transient elastography 23 kPa. Percutaneous liver biopsy performed during visit; histology demonstrated malignant hepatocytes in trabecular patterns with fibrous septa. AFP rose to 190 ng/mL post‑biopsy. Symptoms include lightheadedness, occasional vague melena, and hoarseness. Physical exam revealed scleral icterus, mild ascites, and tenderness over RUQ liver edge. Patient continues antiviral therapy (tenofovir alafenamide, sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) and diuretic regimen (spironolactone 40 mg PO daily, furosemide 20 mg PO daily).

Vital Signs:
BP 158/90, HR 92, Temp 98.6 °F, RR 16, Weight 190 lb, BMI 32, SpO₂ 98%.

Assessment:
1. Probable HCC (3.5‑cm, arterial enhancement, washout) in a cirrhotic liver.
2. Moderate portal hypertension per elastography.
3. Possible GI bleeding source (melena) and variceal risk.

Plan:
- Discuss therapeutic options with patient: transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) or radiofrequency ablation (RFA) as initial locoregional therapy; consider liver transplantation if within Milan criteria and candidacy after multidisciplinary evaluation.
- Continue antiviral therapy and portal hypertension management.
- Repeat AFP and liver function tests in 2 weeks to assess post‑biopsy fluctuation and therapeutic response.
- Schedule multidisciplinary tumor board review within 1 week.
- Counsel patient on signs of decompensation and immediate reporting of worsening ascites, melena, or dizziness.

Medications: 
- Tenofovir alafenamide
- Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir
- Spironolactone 40 mg PO daily
- Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
- Metformin 850 mg BID
- Lisinopril 20 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly

Follow‑up Recommendations:
- Return to hepatology clinic in 2 weeks for repeat labs and clinical assessment.
- Arrange for endoscopy to evaluate varices if indicated.
- Contact clinic at any sign of severe GI bleeding, jaundice, or sudden decompensation.
- Consider early transplant referral if within eligibility.
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule multidisciplinary tumor board review within 1 week
Arrange repeat AFP and LFTs in 2 weeks
Consider liver transplant evaluation if within criteria
Monitor for signs of GI bleeding and ascites complications
Follow up in hepatology clinic in 2 weeks

c9a05975.content:
2024-02-15 – Tumor Board Discussion – Treatment Planning for Hepatic Lesion

Patient: 65‑year‑old male with chronic hepatitis B and C, cirrhosis (Child‑Pugh B), portal hypertension, metabolic syndrome, obesity, type 2 DM, HTN, HLD.  Current medications: Tenofovir alafenamide, Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir, Spironolactone 40 mg PO daily, Furosemide 20 mg PO daily, Metformin 850 mg BID, Lisinopril 20 mg daily, Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly.

Chief Complaint / Reason for Visit

- Multidisciplinary tumor board convened to formulate first‑line treatment plan for a 3.5‑cm hypoechoic hepatic lesion (segment 6) identified on prior contrast‑US.  Patient has ongoing fatigue and mild abdominal discomfort.

History & Physical

- AFP 190 ng/mL, INR 1.4, albumin low, mild ascites, scleral icterus present, no worsening jaundice.  Weight 190 lb, BMI 32.
- BP 160/88, HR 90, RR 16, Temp 98.3 °F, SpO₂ 98%.
- Physical exam: mild ascites, RUQ tenderness, scleral icterus.  No hepatic encephalopathy noted.

Assessment

1. Suspected hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – most likely given hepatic mass, rising AFP, cirrhosis, and risk factors.
2. Child‑Pugh B cirrhosis – dictates cautious use of cytotoxic agents and limits resection options.
3. Low‑dose kinase inhibitor (e.g., sorafenib or lenvatinib) as adjunct therapy considered to improve survival if interventional or surgical treatment not feasible.
4. Metabolic syndrome and obesity – contributors to hepatic steatosis and progression of liver disease; weight reduction may improve liver function.

Plan

- Order CT angiography of the liver in 3 days to characterize lesion vascularity, size, and relationship to major vessels.
- Schedule percutaneous ablation/ transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) within 2 weeks of CT result, contingent on adequate hepatic reserve (Child‑Pugh B).
- Initiate low‑dose sorafenib 50 mg PO twice daily as supplemental therapy, to be started after confirming no contraindication on imaging and labs.
- Continue current diuretic regimen for ascites.  Monitor electrolytes, renal function, and urine output.
- Referral to dietitian to tailor a calorie‑restricted diet (≈1200–1500 kcal/day) with adequate protein and micronutrients; recommend vitamin K and B12 supplementation to minimize encephalopathy risk.
- Maintain antiviral therapy for HBV and HCV; review LFTs and antiviral drug levels in 2 weeks.
- Counsel about avoiding alcohol, hepatotoxins, and ensuring vaccination for hepatitis A.
- Advise patient to report any new neurological symptoms, encephalopathy signs (confusion, drowsiness, altered gait), or worsening ascites.

Follow‑up

- Return visit in 2 weeks for procedure scheduling and review of CT angiography results.
- Repeat AFP, CBC, CMP, INR, and platelet count at next visit.
- Interventional radiology to confirm suitability for ablation; proceed as per tumor board recommendation.
- Continue multidisciplinary coordination (oncology, gastroenterology, radiology, palliative care, dietitian, and primary care).
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule CT angiography in 3 days
Plan percutaneous ablation or TACE within 2 weeks contingent on imaging
Start low‑dose sorafenib 50 mg PO BID after confirming no contraindication
Continue diuretics and monitor electrolytes
Refer to dietitian for weight‑loss program
Repeat AFP, CBC, CMP, INR, and platelets in 2 weeks
Return visit in 2 weeks for procedure scheduling
Advise patient to report any signs of encephalopathy or worsening ascites
Maintain multidisciplinary team coordination

c9a05975.content:
Chief Complaint: First TACE procedure for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in liver segment 6.

History of Present Illness: 65‑year‑old male with a complex hepatobiliary history (HBV, HCV, cirrhosis, metabolic syndrome, obesity, DM2, HTN, hyperlipidemia, portal hypertension, and HCC) underwent his first transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) on 2024-04-01. Under fluoroscopic guidance, a doxorubicin‑loaded emulsion and calibrated embolic microspheres were delivered into the segment 6 feeding artery. Post‑embolization CT demonstrated successful selective occlusion with no extrahepatic spread. The patient developed classic post‑TACE syndrome, presenting with low‑grade fever (100.2 °F), mild diffuse abdominal discomfort, and nausea. Symptom control was achieved with acetaminophen and ondansetron; advised adequate hydration, light meals, and monitoring for fever spikes.

Past Medical History: Chronic hepatitis B & C, cirrhosis, HCC (segment 6), portal hypertension, moderate ascites, diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, obesity.

Medications: Tenofovir alafenamide, sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, sorafenib 50 mg PO BID, spironolactone 40 mg PO daily, furosemide 20 mg PO daily, metformin 850 mg BID, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. No known drug allergies.

Review of Systems: Positive for mild right‑upper‑quadrant tenderness, post‑procedure nausea; otherwise unremarkable.

Physical Examination: Vitals: BP 140/78 mm Hg, HR 94 bpm, Temp 100.2 °F, RR 18 /min, O₂ sat 99 % on room air, weight 190 lb, BMI 32. HEENT: scleral icterus present. Abdomen: mild ascites, tender RUQ, no hepatomegaly. No jaundice or edema noted.

Imaging: Post‑procedure contrast‑enhanced CT – selective embolization of the segment 6 HCC with no evidence of extrahepatic spread or biliary injury.

Laboratory: AFP 460 ng/mL (up from 190 ng/mL six weeks ago). LFTs stable compared to baseline; INR 1.4, albumin 3.2 g/dL.

Assessment: 1. HCC – segment 6, treated with TACE, currently showing post‑procedure changes. 2. Elevated AFP trend, may reflect disease activity or early treatment response. 3. Post‑TACE syndrome – resolving. 4. Moderate ascites secondary to portal hypertension, controlled with diuretics. 5. Cirrhosis with compensated liver function.

Plan:
- Continue current medication regimen (including antiviral and sorafenib). No dose adjustment of sorafenib currently warranted.
- Maintain spironolactone and furosemide for ascites; monitor weight, sodium, and electrolytes.
- Advise patient to maintain adequate oral hydration, avoid heavy meals, and report fever >101 °F, abdominal pain > pain medication manageable, or signs of infection.
- Repeat AFP, CBC, CMP, INR, and LFTs in 4 weeks; repeat abdominal CT with contrast and possible liver‑artery angiography in 8 weeks or sooner if clinical decline.
- Schedule multidisciplinary tumor board review at next clinic visit to discuss potential repeat TACE, radiofrequency ablation, or transition to advanced systemic therapy if AFP trend suggests progression.
- Educate regarding signs of hepatic decompensation (increasing abdominal distention, confusion, altered consciousness, bruising) – patient to call 24/7 if noted.

Disposition: Patient discharged home with prescription refills and scheduled return visit in 4 weeks.

Follow‑up Recommendations: See section in Plan.
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule clinic visit in 4 weeks for labs (AFP, LFTs, INR, CBC)
Repeat abdominal CT with contrast in 8 weeks or sooner if clinical deterioration
Continue current diuretics; monitor sodium & weight
Maintain current sorafenib dosing; assess tolerance
Discuss potential repeat TACE or alternative systemic therapy at tumor board
Patient education on signs of hepatic decompensation and when to seek urgent care

c9a05975.content:
Chief Complaint: 65‑year‑old male presents with worsening abdominal distention, lower‑extremity edema, speech difficulty, and episodic confusion consistent with hepatic encephalopathy.

History of Present Illness: The patient has a long history of chronic hepatitis B and C with cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and hepatocellular carcinoma (AFP 460 ng/mL) that has progressed despite multiple TACE procedures. He began sorafenib 6 weeks ago at 400 mg BID; however, AST/ALT rose to 120/105 IU/L, and the patient reported significant nausea, prompting discontinuation of sorafenib. He reports refractory ascites despite 20 mg furosemide and 100 mg spironolactone daily, now with additional torsemide 20 mg BID. Laboratory evaluation shows serum creatinine 1.5 mg/dL, rising to 1.5 mg/dL diurnally from a baseline of 0.8–1.0 mg/dL, bilirubin 4.3 mg/dL, albumin 1.8 g/dL, INR 2.5, and mild hepatitis flare (AST 120, ALT 105).

Physical Examination: Scleral icterus; marked ascites (tender, shifting dullness); lower‑extremity pitting edema; mild hepatic encephalopathy (speech slowed, confusion). Vital signs: BP 170/90 mm Hg, HR 98 bpm, Temp 98.4 °F, RR 16/min, O₂ sat 98 %, weight 190 lb, BMI 32.

Procedures: Upper GI endoscopy revealed large esophageal varices with red‑sign stigmata; four bands were placed. Bedside ultrasound confirmed large ascitic fluid collection.

Assessment: 1. Refractory ascites secondary to advanced cirrhosis with portal hypertension. 2. Hepatocellular carcinoma with rising AFP and recent TACE. 3. Mild hepatic encephalopathy. 4. Hepatorenal syndrome type 1 (serum creatinine rise, low albumin, hyperdynamic circulation). 5. Sorafenib‑related hepatocellular injury causing transaminase elevation.

Plan:
1. Continue current diuretics: torsemide 20 mg BID and furosemide 20 mg daily; titrate every 48–72 h based on weight and fluid balance. 2. Maintain spironolactone 100 mg daily and prednisone taper (low dose). 3. Hold sorafenib permanently due to toxicity. 4. Continue surveillance for HCC progression with AFP and imaging (contrast‑enhanced CT in 8 weeks or sooner if clinical changes). 5. Consider TIPS placement if ascites remains refractory and synthetic function improves; however, current synthetic indices (INR 2.5, albumin 1.8 g/dL) make this a high‑risk option. 6. Refuse peritoneovenous shunt due to poor synthetic reserve. 7. Intensify anti‑bleeding prophylaxis: continue proton pump inhibitor (if not already) and repeat endoscopy in 6 weeks. 8. Refer for transplant evaluation. 9. Palliative care continued to address goals of care. 10. Monitor renal function: daily urine output and serum creatinine, adjust diuretics accordingly.

Follow‑Up Recommendations: • Repeat LFTs, INR, CMP, bilirubin, and creatinine weekly. • Repeat abdominal ultrasound (or CT) in 4 weeks or earlier if worsening fluid overload. • Schedule transplant clinic visit within 2 weeks. • Follow‑up with palliative care next week to discuss symptom control and advance directives. • Continue low‑dose prednisone; repeat daily hepatic panel to ensure no rebound transaminase surge. • Encourage protein‑restricted diet (1.0–1.2 g/kg body weight) with sodium restriction (<2 g/day). • Review variceal bleeding risk with repeat EGD in 6 weeks.

Medications: Metformin 850 mg BID, Lisinopril 20 mg daily, Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, Spironolactone 100 mg daily, Furosemide 20 mg daily, Torsemide 20 mg BID, Prednisone taper (low dose).
diagnoses_considered: Refractory ascites due to advanced cirrhosis
follow_up_recommendations: Repeat LFTs, INR, CMP, bilirubin, and creatinine weekly
Repeat abdominal ultrasound (or CT) in 4 weeks or sooner if worsening fluid overload
Schedule transplant clinic visit within 2 weeks
Follow-up with palliative care next week
Continue low‑dose prednisone; repeat daily hepatic panel
Encourage protein‑restricted diet (1.0–1.2 g/kg) with sodium restriction (<2 g/day)
Review variceal bleeding risk with repeat EGD in 6 weeks

c9a05975.content:
Chief Complaint: End‑of‑life care and hospice referral. 

History of Present Illness: Mr. Lewis, 65‑year‑old male with advanced cirrhosis from chronic hepatitis B and C, presents after admission by hospice for worsening abdominal distention and a mild head injury sustained in a fall. CT abdomen with contrast showed a 4.2‑cm hepatic lesion invading a branch of the portal vein; no extrahepatic metastasis was seen. Laboratory studies indicate severe coagulopathy (INR 2.5), profound hypoalbuminemia (1.8 g/dL), and rising bilirubin. He can no longer tolerate oral intake. The palliative team activated a low‑dose IV morphine infusion for pain and discussed goals of care with his family. The consensus was to discontinue all active oncologic therapy and transition to hospice care at home. Home hospice personnel have been contacted and a short‑term pain plan and family counseling have been arranged.

Past Medical History: Chronic hepatitis B & C, cirrhosis, metabolic syndrome, iron overload, severe ascites, portal hypertension, hepatorenal syndrome, severe hepatic encephalopathy. Prior interventions: TACE, sorafenib (held), variceal ligation, refractory ascites, multiple diuretic adjustments.

Physical Examination: Scleral icterus present. Abdomen tense with significant ascites; soft but tender to palpation. Speech weak, patient confused. Low‑grade fever 99 °F. Respiratory rate 20/min. Oxygen sat 97 % on room air.

Assessment:
1. Decompensated cirrhosis with HCC stage IV (portal vein invasion). 2. Severe hepatic encephalopathy. 3. Diuretic‑resistant ascites. 4. Coagulopathy and hypoalbuminemia. 5. Patient wishes to pursue comfort care.

Plan:
• Continue low‑dose IV morphine infusion (target 0.1 mg/h with titration to effect).  • Maintain current diuretic regimen: spironolactone 100 mg PO daily, furosemide 20 mg PO daily.  • Continue lisinopril 20 mg PO daily for chronic hypertension.  • Discontinue all oncologic therapy (sorafenib, TACE) and discontinue prednisone.  • Continue routine labs: CBC (already obtained), CMP, LFTs, INR, albumin; repeat every 4–6 weeks or sooner if clinical change.  • Monitor for signs of worsening hepatic encephalopathy, renal function, and ascites.  • Provide family education on hospice care, symptom management, and comfort measures.  • Arrange for home hospice team visits: initial rapid assessment, then weekly follow‑ups or sooner as needed.

Prognosis: Due to advanced liver disease with portal vein tumor thrombus and multi‑system decompensation, life expectancy is limited. Comfort care focus will continue.

Disposition: Transition to home hospice care with symptom‑oriented management and ongoing support for patient and family.
diagnoses_considered: Hepatocellular carcinoma with portal vein invasion (Stage IV)
follow_up_recommendations: Contact home hospice team for rapid assessment and weekly visits
Monitor pain and adjust morphine infusion as needed
Assess for worsening encephalopathy and renal function
Provide caregiver support and psychosocial counseling
Update hospice team on any clinical changes

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: panic attacks and mood symptoms.

History of Present Illness: Marcus, a 24‑year‑old male, presented to the campus health clinic after a sudden panic attack in a lecture. He reported chest tightness, racing heart, and a sense of impending doom. The attack resolved spontaneously but was followed by persistent low mood and insomnia. He denies recent stressors beyond routine academic demands. He reports daily cannabis use (3‑4 joints/day) and acknowledges it may worsen anxiety.

Past Psychiatric History: Major depressive disorder, anxiety disorder, cannabis use disorder. No current psychotropic medication. Parental divorce, low socioeconomic status, history of childhood trauma (father abandonment).

Review of Systems: Positive for panic attacks, insomnia, low mood, anhedonia, nausea. Negative for chest pain aside from anxiety, fever, weight loss, urinary or bowel changes.

Physical Examination: Vitals stable (BP 120/78 mmHg, HR 94 bpm, Temp 36.7°C, RR 18/min, SpO2 98%). Tense neck musculature noted. Cardiovascular exam normal. No focal neurological deficits.

Assessment: Panic disorder with moderate‑severe depression and ongoing cannabis use, contributing to anxiety and insomnia. No acute medical cause identified.

Plan:
1. Start sertraline 50 mg PO daily. Titrate up by 25 mg every 2 weeks if tolerated.
2. Refer to outpatient cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with four scheduled sessions.
3. Counsel on reducing cannabis use; implement a gradual taper plan to minimize rebound anxiety.
4. Recommend sleep hygiene education (consistent bedtime, limiting screen time, caffeine restriction).
5. Monitor for SSRI side effects (nausea, insomnia, GI upset). Provide guidance on reporting.
6. Follow‑up in 4 weeks to assess medication response and psychiatric status.
7. Encourage daily mood and panic attack diary.

The patient expressed motivation to engage in treatment and reported slight improvement in panic frequency after five days of sertraline.

Plan is aligned with current guidelines for panic disorder and major depressive disorder in young adults.
follow_up_recommendations: Follow‑up visit in 4 weeks
Continue CBT sessions
Monitor mood and panic attacks
Reduce cannabis use
Discuss side effects
Adjust sertraline dose if needed
Implement sleep hygiene strategies

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: Worsening mood and behavioral changes.

History of Present Illness: Patient is a 24‑year‑old male with a past psychiatric history of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), Cannabis Use Disorder (CUD), and Generalized Anxiety Disorder. He presents for a routine follow‑up and reports a continued low mood, increased irritability, poor sleep, and frequent concentration difficulties. He also reports intermittent faint auditory comments (“someone behind me”). A brief symptom questionnaire yielded a score of 18, consistent with moderate depressive symptoms.

Past Psychiatric Treatment: The patient has been on sertraline 50 mg PO daily for MDD. He discontinued due to inadequate response and was partially non‑adherent with counseling appointments. He remains active in cannabis use (≈2 joints/day) and reports a 4‑kg recent weight loss.

Mental Status Examination: Aged 24, male, alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Poor eye contact; speech is slowed and somewhat hesitant. Affect is constricted, mood described as “low.” No suicidal or homicidal ideation reported. No overt psychotic symptoms beyond the occasional auditory comment. Insight is limited; judgment somewhat impaired likely related to cannabis use.

Vital Signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, Temp 36.5°C, RR 16/min, SpO₂ 99% on room air.

Assessment: The patient appears to be experiencing an exacerbation of his MDD with emerging subclinical psychotic symptoms. Possible contributors include: inadequate antidepressant coverage, ongoing cannabis use, and possible medication side effect from sertraline. Differential diagnoses include major depressive disorder, substance‑induced mood disorder (cannabis‑related), acute psychotic episode secondary to substance use, and anxiety disorder.

Plan:
1. Discontinue sertraline immediately.
2. Initiate citalopram 20 mg PO once daily. Titrate by 20 mg increments every 2 weeks up to 40 mg as tolerated, monitoring for serotonin syndrome.
3. Add low‑dose risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly for emerging auditory phenomena and irritability. Educate patient on sedation, orthostatic hypotension, and extrapyramidal side effects; advise to monitor and report significant sedation.
4. Counsel on cannabis cessation; discuss risk of psychosis and worsening depression. Encourage gradual taper with support.
5. Emphasize sleep hygiene: consistent bedtime, limiting caffeine, use of relaxation techniques.
6. Repeat CBC, CMP, liver enzymes, and urine drug screen in 2 weeks.
7. Schedule a weekly psychiatric follow‑up via telehealth or office visit; instruct to seek urgent care if symptoms of psychosis, suicidal ideation, or marked agitation occur.
8. Provide handout on medication adherence and side effect monitoring.

The patient demonstrated insight into medication changes and acknowledges anxiety around side effects. He declined immediate psychotherapy but agreed to engage with CBT in the future. Follow‑up is arranged for next week.
follow_up_recommendations: Weekly psychiatric follow‑up (telehealth or office)
Repeat labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, UDS) in 2 weeks
Seek urgent care if symptoms worsen (psychosis, suicidal ideation, agitation)
Adherence education and cannabis cessation counseling

cac4bbf5.content:
Subjective: 22-year-old male presents to urgent‑care with acute auditory hallucinations and agitation. He reports hearing voices shouting 'someone behind him' and is upset with a bus driver. He appears suspicious of staff, tense, and difficult to cooperate.

Objective: Vital signs: BP 140/90, HR 106, Temp 37.2°C, RR 20, SpO2 97%. Physical exam notable for mild hand tremor, tense posture, inappropriate affect. No focal neurologic deficits. Observed for restlessness.

Assessment: Acute exacerbation of emerging psychosis likely triggered by recent heavy cannabis use. Differential includes cannabis‑induced psychosis, major depressive disorder with psychotic features, and early schizophrenia. Labs: CBC normal, urine drug screen positive for THC.

Plan: Administered 20 mg IM haloperidol, observed 45 minutes with mild calming, no resolution of hallucinations. Transferred to ED for further evaluation. Continue current psychiatric medications (citalopram 20 mg PO daily, risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly). Consider increasing citalopram up to 40 mg pending ED evaluation. Provide safety counseling, encourage cannabis cessation. Arrange follow‑up with psychiatry within 48 hours and urgent‑care PCP within 1 week. Document discharge summary from ED.
diagnoses_considered: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
follow_up_recommendations: Transfer to ED for further evaluation
Continue risperidone 0.5 mg nightly

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: Severe psychosis after cannabis binge.

History of Present Illness:

A 22‑year‑old male with a history of Major Depressive Disorder, Cannabis Use Disorder, and Psychosis presents to the ED with acute auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions, and motor agitation following a heavy cannabis binge (10+ joints) approximately 48 hours ago.  Patient reports voices instructing him to “get out” and believes strangers are watching him through surveillance cameras.  He appears confused about the time of day and repeatedly asks staff if they are “surveillance cameras.”  He is oriented to person only, exhibits pressured and fluent speech, and is mildly agitated.

Past Psychiatric History:
- Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) – Current treatment citalopram 20 mg PO daily.
- Psychotic episodes with auditory hallucinations – Risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly.
- Cannabis Use Disorder – 10+ joints binge 48 hrs ago.

Past Medical History:
- Weight loss 5 kg over 2 months.
- Missed outpatient appointments.

Medications (current):
- Citalopram 20 mg PO daily
- Risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly
- Olanzapine 10 mg PO nightly (recently initiated)

Allergies: None

Review of Systems:
- Positive for auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions, agitation.
- Negative for fever, chest pain, shortness of breath, rash, weakness.

Physical Examination:
- Vitals: BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 112 bpm, Temp 36.9 °C, RR 18 /min, SpO₂ 96%.
- General: Alert only to person, mildly anxious, agitated.
- HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
- Respiratory: Clear to auscultation.
- Neurologic: Oriented to person only, fluent but pressured speech.
- Psych: Auditory hallucinations present.

Assessments & Plan:

1. Acute cannabis‑induced psychosis (most likely given temporal relationship and positive urinalysis).  The psychosis is likely exacerbated by current antipsychotic levels and may be insufficient.  
   • Continue olanzapine 10 mg PO nightly, monitor for sedation and weight gain.
   • Consider increasing olanzapine to 15 mg PO nightly after 48 hrs if symptoms persist.
   • Continue citalopram 20 mg PO daily; consider titrating to 40 mg after safety evaluation.

2. Substance‑induced mania / psychosis differential, but less likely given the absence of mood elevation.

3. Ongoing cannabis use disorder: Reinforce abstinence counseling, provide referral to substance diversion program.

4. Elevated liver enzymes (AST 55, ALT 78) likely secondary to chronic cannabis use and olanzapine; repeat LFTs in 2 weeks.

5. Inpatient observation unit: admit to inpatient psychiatric unit for 48‑hour observation to monitor mental status and medication response.

6. Safety: Provide secure environment, monitor for self‑harm risk.

7. Follow‑up: 
   • Discharge to inpatient psychiatric unit with medication adherence education.
   • Weekly outpatient psychiatry visit after discharge.
   • Reinforce adherence to risperidone and olanzapine; assess tolerability.
   • Counsel on cannabis cessation; offer CBT and group support.
   • Monitor vitals and hepatic panel.

Disposition:
Admitted to inpatient psychiatric unit for 48‑hour observation with ongoing treatment.

Prepared by: [Emergency Psychiatrist]
diagnoses_considered: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
Acute psychosis secondary to substance use
follow_up_recommendations: Inpatient psychiatric unit observation 48 hrs
Medication adherence education
Continue olanzapine nightly
Monitor liver enzymes
Weekly psychiatry follow‑up outpatient
Counseling for cannabis cessation
Re‑evaluate citalopram dose
Consider haloperidol taper if needed

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: Re‑hospitalization following self‑medication with liquid haloperidol. 

History of Present Illness: 23‑year‑old male with a known history of schizophrenia, major depressive disorder, anxiety disorder, cannabis use disorder and ongoing suicidal ideation was brought to the psychiatric unit after an emergency response indicated a possible self‑harm attempt.  He reported injecting a half‑dose (0.5 mL) of liquid haloperidol into his left forearm 12 hours prior to arrival.  He noted subjective improvement after injection but continued to report paranoid thoughts (“watching” by neighbors), self‑injury thoughts, and suicidal ideation.  He had not taken any scheduled medication for five days, was in a halfway house overnight, and had a superficial left forearm laceration from a self‑inflicted injury. 

On presentation he was disoriented, confused, pale, and exhibited mild bilateral tremor.  Vital signs were stable: BP 102/68 mm Hg, HR 98 bpm, Temp 37.0 °C, RR 18 /min, SpO₂ 97 %.  A physical exam revealed a superficial laceration on the left forearm and mild tremor.  Labs showed elevated liver enzymes: AST 105 U/L, ALT 88 U/L, ALP 80 U/L, GGT 45 U/L.  CBC and CMP otherwise normal.  Urine drug screen was positive for THC, negative for benzodiazepines.

Assessment: Acute agitation and psychosis most likely secondary to a combination of uncontrolled schizophrenia, abrupt discontinuation of antipsychotics, cannabis use, and accidental over‑dose of haloperidol.  Co‑existing acute iatrogenic drug toxicity (possible haloperidol toxicity and hepatotoxicity) is considered.  The patient remains at high suicide risk despite initial response to sedation.

Plan:
1. Medication:
   - Continue Olanzapine 10 mg PO daily (no change).
   - Initiate Quetiapine 200 mg PO at bedtime to augment antipsychotic control.
   - Lorazepam 2 mg PO q6h PRN for agitation.
   - Continue existing Citalopram 20 mg PO daily; consider titration to 30–40 mg after safety review.
   - Risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly remains unchanged.
   - Discontinue IV propofol; use oral agents moving forward.
2. Monitoring:
   - Daily vitals and mental status.
   - Repeat LFTs in 3 days.
   - Monitor for extrapyramidal symptoms.
3. Safety:
   - Formal suicide risk assessment; safety plan in place.
   - Continuous observation.
4. Psychotherapy:
   - Initiate individual therapy focusing on coping strategies and medication adherence.
   - Group therapy for substance use education.
5. Social:
   - Social work scheduled to discuss housing stability and potential discharge planning.
6. Follow‑up:
   - Psychiatric evaluation daily.
   - Discuss possible dose increase of Olanzapine if psychosis persists.
   - Review liver function tests and adjust antipsychotic dosing as needed.
   - Family meeting to reinforce support and safety.

Disposition: Admitted to inpatient psychiatric unit for continued stabilization, medication management, and safety monitoring.

Prognosis: With consistent medication adherence, therapy, and monitoring of liver function, prognosis is guarded but can improve with sustained support and treatment compliance.
diagnoses_considered: Acute psychotic episode secondary to cannabis use
Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
follow_up_recommendations: Daily psychiatric evaluation
Monitor for EPM symptoms
Repeat LFTs in 3 days
Individual & group therapy for substance use
Family meeting for safety planning
Social work visit for housing
Consider Olanzapine dose increase if psychosis persists

cac4bbf5.content:
Patient is a 23‑year‑old male with a complex psychiatric history of schizophrenia, major depressive disorder, anxiety disorder and cannabis use disorder. He was discharged 10 days ago from an inpatient unit on olanzapine 10 mg nightly and quetiapine 200 mg bedtime. At this follow‑up, Marcus reports a resurgence of auditory hallucinations that he describes as louder after a recent cannabis binge. He also complains of constipation, 4 kg weight gain over 2 weeks and nausea. He admitted skipping his olanzapine dose for the past 3 days because of sedation.

Vitals are stable: BP 112/70, HR 88, Temp 36.8°C, RR 16, SpO₂ 98%. Physical exam reveals somnolence and mild tremor.

Labs: CBC and CMP are within normal limits. AST 55, ALT 48 (slightly elevated but not clinically concerning), LFTs normal.

Assessment: 
- Acute exacerbation of psychosis likely precipitated by cannabis use and recent olanzapine discontinuation. 
- Medication side effects: sedation, constipation, weight gain, nausea.
- Possible olanzapine withdrawal contributing to symptom relapse.

Plan: 
- Adjust antipsychotic regimen: olanzapine reduced to 5 mg nightly; quetiapine increased to 300 mg bedtime. 
- Continue citalopram 20 mg daily and risperidone 0.5 mg nightly. 
- Provide ondansetron 4 mg PRN for nausea. 
- Counsel on cannabis harm‑reduction; referral to local program. 
- Discuss side‑effect management and set realistic goals for dose titration. 
- Implement weekly phone check‑in to monitor adherence, side effects and symptom trajectory. 
- Re‑order CBC, CMP and LFTs in 4 weeks to monitor metabolic and hepatic impact of medication changes.

The patient expressed frustration with side effects but agreed to the cautious taper plan. He is expected to return to clinic in 2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen.
diagnoses_considered: Medication side effects: constipation, weight gain, nausea
follow_up_recommendations: Weekly phone check‑in to assess adherence and symptoms
Re‑order CBC, CMP, LFTs in 4 weeks
Monitor weight and metabolic parameters
Refer to local cannabis harm‑reduction program
Consider cautious olanzapine taper if symptoms remain controlled

cac4bbf5.content:
I/We saw the patient on admission following a police call for a self‑harm attempt. The patient, a 23‑year‑old male with a history of schizophrenia, cannabis use disorder, major depressive disorder, and prior suicidal ideation, presented with disorganized speech, pressured thinking, and delusions that the city council was surveilling him. He stated he was going to do the voices' job. On exam he was tachycardic (HR 102) with BP 98/60, RR 20, SpO2 97%, and had mild tremor. He was mildly sedated after 25 mg IM haloperidol, which reduced agitation but did not terminate hallucinations.

Work‑up: CBC and CMP were normal except AST 81 U/L and ALT 70 U/L; GGT 49 U/L. CT head was unremarkable. The psychiatric team initiated a safety plan, started Risperidone 4 mg PO daily, increased Olanzapine to 7 mg PO nightly, and introduced Quetiapine 200 mg bedtime. Lorazepam 2 mg PRN was kept for agitation. The patient was placed under observation and began individual therapy focusing on coping with auditory hallucinations. He was counselled on medication adherence and cannabis abstinence.

Assessment: Acute schizophrenic psychosis with severe auditory hallucinations and suicidal ideation likely precipitated by cannabis binge and medication non‑adherence. Risk of future self‑harm; elevated LFTs possibly drug‑related. Patient is currently stable after 11 days of inpatient care and was discharged on Olanzapine 7 mg nightly, Quetiapine 200 mg bedtime, Risperidone 4 mg daily, Lorazepam 2 mg PRN.

Plan: • Continue discharge medication regimen. • Obtain CBC, CMP, LFTs in 4 weeks to monitor hepatic function. • Arrange outpatient psychiatric follow‑up within 5 days, ideally within 1 week. • Maintain safety plan: call crisis line if suicidal thoughts recur, avoid self‑harm tools. • Refer to substance‑use counseling for cannabis use disorder. • Encourage engagement in CBT for psychosis and depression. • Monitor for extrapyramidal side‑effects and weight changes. • Patient to contact the unit immediately if agitation or suicidal ideation increases.
diagnoses_considered: Acute schizophrenic psychosis with active auditory hallucinations
Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
follow_up_recommendations: Discharge to home with outpatient follow‑up in 1 week
Arrange outpatient psychiatry appointment within 5 days
Continue safety plan: crisis line call, remove hazardous items
Maintain medication adherence, especially antipsychotics
Counseling on cannabis cessation and substance use disorder treatment
Monitor liver function tests in 4 weeks
Follow up labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs
Encourage support network involvement
Consider psychotherapy (CBT for psychosis and suicide prevention)
If suicidal ideation worsens, seek immediate care

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: Follow‑up and medication regimen adjustment.

History of Present Illness: 24‑year‑old male with a history of schizophrenia, cannabis use disorder, and major depressive disorder presents for a routine follow‑up. He reports hearing voices loudly, feeling paranoid about a perceived surveillance system, and experiencing a recent episode of believing a “shadow organization” intended to “wipe him out.” He also reports insomnia and irritability. The patient missed his risperidone dose for the past 2 days. He arrives late due to transportation issues and reported recent cannabis use.

Past Psychiatric History: Multiple prior admissions for cannabis‑induced psychosis, self‑harm, and acute agitation. Current medication regimen includes olanzapine 7 mg nightly, quetiapine 200 mg bedtime, risperidone 4 mg daily, and lorazepam 2 mg PRN. He has a history of inconsistent medication adherence and weight gain.

Review of Systems: Positive for auditory hallucinations, insomnia, irritability, weight gain, tremor, slowed speech. Negative for suicidality at this visit.

Physical Examination: Alert, oriented, normal affect but slowed speech. Vital signs stable. Notable findings: alopecia over scalp (olanzapine‑related), mild tremor in upper extremities. No acute distress.

Labs: Lipid panel – LDL 140 mg/dL, HDL 45 mg/dL, TG 250 mg/dL; Thyroid panel – T4 0.7 ng/dL (low); Urine drug screen – THC positive. Weight 85 kg, BMI 29.5.

Assessment: 1) Schizophrenia – relapsing psychotic symptoms likely exacerbated by recent missed risperidone dose and continued cannabis use.
2) Antipsychotic‑induced metabolic syndrome – significant hyperlipidemia and weight gain.
3) Antipsychotic‑induced extrapyramidal side effect – mild upper‑extremity tremor.
4) Possible subclinical hypothyroidism – low free T4.
5) Cannabis‑related psychosis – positive urine screen.

Plan:
- Adjust risperidone to 2 mg daily (reduction from 4 mg). 
- Add aripiprazole 10 mg PO daily to offset sedation and provide partial agonism.
- Continue olanzapine 7 mg nightly and quetiapine 200 mg bedtime. 
- Lorazepam 2 mg PO PRN for agitation.
- Repeat lipid panel and thyroid panel in 2 weeks. If T4 remains low, consider levothyroxine therapy in consultation with endocrinology.
- Counsel on cannabis cessation; offer nicotine replacement and behavioral strategies.
- Reinforce CBT psycho‑education; schedule bi‑weekly CBT sessions.
- Safety plan: Discuss coping strategies, crisis contact number, and when to seek urgent care.
- Arrange 2‑week telehealth check‑in and in‑person visit in 6 weeks for medication titration review.

Follow‑up Recommendations: 1) Two‑week telehealth appointment to assess symptom control and medication tolerance.
2) Discuss safety plan and cannabis abstinence progress.
3) Monitor weight and metabolic parameters at each visit.
4) Review thyroid results; initiate levothyroxine if indicated.
5) Continue CBT sessions and psychiatric follow‑up every 4 weeks thereafter.
follow_up_recommendations: Telehealth check‑in in 2 weeks
In‑person visit in 6 weeks for medication titration
CBT sessions bi‑weekly
Repeat lipid and thyroid labs in 2 weeks
Safety plan review
Counseling on cannabis cessation

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: Missed appointments, increased risk, plan for crisis.

Subjective: 24‑year‑old male, Marcus, presented early, fatigued, and reporting a new crisis: he planned to "go to the park" and kill himself because he believes the city is orchestrating his death. He denies intent to act but admits to a plan. Marcus’s baseline history includes schizophrenia, cannabis use disorder, major depressive disorder, and prior self‑harm ideation. He reports severe auditory hallucinations, insomnia, and agitation.

Objective: Vitals – BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, Temp 36.7°C, RR 18/min, SpO₂ 97%. Examination reveals tremor, weight gain (~8 kg since last visit), poor hygiene, and poor insight. He is alert but restless.

Assessment: Acute suicidal ideation with a plan but no current intent; severe psychotic symptoms likely exacerbated by recent cannabis use and medication non‑adherence; elevated liver enzymes (AST 85, ALT 78, GGT 49 U/L) likely related to poly‑antipsychotic regimen; acute agitation managed with IM haloperidol.

Plan: 1) Admit to psychiatric observation unit for 48 h, institute safety plan and short‑term CBT focusing on crisis management.
2) Medication adjustments: continue Olanzapine 7 mg PO nightly, Quetiapine 300 mg PO QHS (up from 200 mg), Risperidone 2 mg PO daily, add Aripiprazole 10 mg PO daily for augmentation, maintain Lorazepam 2 mg PO PRN for agitation.
3) Continue IM Haloperidol 10 mg as needed; reassess need for future use.
4) Monitor labs: CBC, CMP, LFT panel, repeat AST/ALT in 1 week.
5) Provide a crisis hotline number, ensure transportation assistance for appointments, and arrange daily pill dispensing through a local pharmacy.
6) Initiate a routine schedule: CBT twice weekly, medication review every 48 h, and a follow‑up outpatient visit in 2 weeks.

Disposition: Patient consented to observation unit, expressed appreciation for support, and agreed to the safety plan.
diagnoses_considered: Acute suicidal ideation with intent plan
Acute hepatic dysfunction from antipsychotic use
follow_up_recommendations: Daily pill dispensing via local pharmacy
Crisis hotline contact details
Follow‑up outpatient visit in 2 weeks
Weekly CBT sessions
Laboratory monitoring: CBC, CMP, LFTs at 1 week
Transportation assistance for appointments
Medication adherence education
Review liver function tests after 1 month

cac4bbf5.content:
Chief Complaint: 24‑year‑old male with acute deterioration and homicidal ideation. Patient was brought to the unit after a police call at an abandoned building. He reported that police were “agents” and that he would “take them down.” He described a city conspiracy and reported auditory hallucinations. He had previously cut his wrists earlier that day. 

History: Known for schizophrenia, cannabis use disorder, major depressive disorder, past self‑harm and homicidal ideation. Current regimen includes olanzapine 7 mg nightly, quetiapine 300 mg bedtime, risperidone 2 mg daily, aripiprazole 10 mg daily, lorazepam 2 mg PO PRN. Has a long history of polypharmacy and multiple recent self‑harm episodes. 

Assessment: The patient exhibited high tremor, rigidity, confusion, sweating, and pronounced agitation, raising concern for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) after a recent intramuscular haloperidol dose (20 mg) and high‑dose antipsychotics. CK was markedly elevated at 1,800 U/L (normal < 200). MRI brain was normal. Liver enzymes AST 80, ALT 70 suggest mild hepatic stress, possibly from poly‑antipsychotic load. Differential diagnosis includes acute psychosis secondary to schizophrenia, medication‑induced NMS, serotonin syndrome (unlikely clinically), substance‑induced psychosis (cannabis positive), major depressive disorder with psychotic features, and schizoaffective disorder. 

Plan and Management: 
1. Continue lorazepam 4 mg IV q2h for sedation and NMS control. 
2. Hold haloperidol IM and consider discontinuing other high‑dose antipsychotics (quetiapine 400 mg QHS, risperidone 2 mg daily, aripiprazole 10 mg daily, olanzapine 7 mg nightly) pending safety reassessment. 
3. Initiate clozapine once baseline labs (CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, urine pregnancy) are obtained; monitor for agranulocytosis. 
4. Daily CK, TSH, CBC, CMP, LFTs; vitals q4h with focus on temperature, HR, BP, RR. 
5. Engage safety council daily; maintain continuous observation. 
6. Conduct daily psychiatric evaluation, safety planning, and psychoeducation regarding cannabis cessation. 
7. Provide antipsychotic taper protocol if NMS resolves; reintroduce low‑dose risperidone or aripiprazole only under close monitoring. 
8. Coordinate with social work for discharge planning: secure outpatient follow‑up, medication teaching, drug screening, and crisis hotline information. 

Disposition: Admitted for high‑acuity monitoring; patient was cooperative after sedation and expressed feeling “stuck” but no active violence.  

Follow‑up: The patient will be re‑evaluated daily in the unit. After stabilization, discharge planning meeting with patient, family/caregiver, and safety council members will be scheduled. Post‑discharge outpatient follow‑up with psychiatry within 48 h, and weekly visits thereafter for medication titration and monitoring.
diagnoses_considered: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
medications: Quetiapine 400 mg PO QHS (hold pending safety review)
Olanzapine 7 mg nightly (hold pending safety review)
Risperidone 2 mg daily (hold pending safety review)
Aripiprazole 10 mg daily (hold pending safety review)
follow_up_recommendations: Daily vitals and neuro checks with emphasis on temperature and rigidity
Daily CK, CBC, CMP, and LFT monitoring
Engage safety council daily; maintain continuous observation
Daily psychiatric evaluation and safety planning
Initiate clozapine after baseline labs and agranulocytosis monitoring
Discharge planning with social work; arrange outpatient follow‑up within 48 h
Psychoeducation on cannabis cessation and medication adherence
Provide crisis hotline and safety plan documentation to patient and family

ce01efdb.content:
Chief Complaint: Persistent cough with hemoptysis. 

History of Present Illness: Mr. Whitaker is a 68‑year‑old male with a long history of COPD, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, asthma, and a prior TB infection with a known cavitary lesion. He presents with a dry cough that worsens at night and occasional whitish sputum. He reports a few streaks of blood in sputum. He denies chest pain, fever, dyspnea beyond baseline, recent travel, or weight loss.

Past Medical History: COPD (15 yrs), Chronic bronchitis, Emphysema, Asthma, History of TB, Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Coronary artery disease (stent placed 20 yrs ago). 

Medications: Albuterol sulfate 90 mcg MDI × 2 puffs PRN, Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 µg (Twisthaler) BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Metformin 500 mg BID, Aspirin 81 mg daily.

Allergies: NKDA.

Social History: Heavy smoker, 60‑year history, 2 packs/day; denies alcohol or illicit drug use.

Review of Systems: Positive for cough, sputum, mild nasal congestion. Negative for chest pain, dyspnea, fever, hematuria, GI bleeding.

Vitals: BP 150/90 mmHg, HR 92/min, RR 18/min, Temp 36.8 °C, SpO₂ 94% RA.

Physical Exam:
- General: Alert, no acute distress.
- HEENT: Clear speech, mild nasal congestion.
- Chest: Decreased breath sounds in left lower lobe, slight wheezing.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, no murmurs.
- Extremities: No edema.
- Skin: No rashes, no clubbing.

Assessment:
1. COPD exacerbation with hemoptysis – likely due to chronic airway inflammation and possible superimposed infection.
2. Possible bronchogenic carcinoma – hemoptysis and heavy smoking history raise concern.
3. Tuberculosis relapse – history of TB and current cavitary-like density on CXR.
4. Bronchiectasis – chronic sputum production.
5. Pulmonary embolism – unexplained hemoptysis, but no risk factors; low likelihood.

Plan:
- Continue existing inhaled therapy (Albuterol PRN, Fluticasone/salmeterol BID). 
- Start oral prednisone 40 mg daily for 5 days to treat COPD exacerbation.
- Advise smoking cessation; refer to smoking cessation program.
- Order chest X‑ray (done – mild, faint density in left lower lobe, emphysematous changes; no effusion).
- Pulmonology referral within 2 weeks for review of imaging, consideration of CT chest and bronchoscopy if suspicion persists.
- Educate patient on red flags (persistent hemoptysis > 3 mL, worsening dyspnea, chest pain) – return to ED.
- Continue blood pressure and diabetes control; monitor BP.
- Follow up in office in 2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen.

Patient understanding confirmed. All decisions explained and documented.
symptoms_reported: Few streaks of blood in sputum
follow_up_recommendations: Pulmonology referral within 2 weeks
Return to ED if hemoptysis > 3 mL or worsening symptoms
Schedule office follow‑up in 2 weeks
Continue smoking cessation program

ce01efdb.content:
Chief Complaint: Follow‑up after chest X‑ray abnormality.

History of Present Illness: 68‑year‑old male (Mr. Whitaker) presents for follow‑up of a new left lower lobe (LLL) density identified on prior chest radiograph. Patient reports persistent dry cough for 3 weeks, occasional hemoptysis, and fatigue. Symptoms are not relieved by his inhaled therapy. No fever, night sweats, weight loss, or dyspnea beyond baseline. Past respiratory illnesses include COPD, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, asthma, and remote tuberculosis. Current medications include albuterol (90 mcg MDI PRN), fluticasone/salmeterol (250/50 µg Twisthaler BID), lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg BID, aspirin 81 mg daily, and a 5‑day course of prednisone 40 mg daily that started 2 days ago.

Review of Systems: Respiratory: cough, occasional hemoptysis, no wheeze worsening. Constitutional: fatigue, no weight loss. Cardiovascular: no chest pain.

Physical Examination:
- Vital signs: BP 148/88 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, RR 20 /min, Temp 36.7 °C, SpO₂ 93 % RA.
- General: Alert, no acute distress.
- Lungs: mild bilateral wheezes, prolonged expiratory phase, no crackles, no clubbing.
- Heart: regular rhythm, no murmurs.
- Abdomen: soft, non‑tender, no hepatosplenomegaly.
- Extremities: no edema.

Baseline spirometry: FEV₁ 1.4 L (45 % predicted), FEV₁/FVC 55 %. No improvement after short‑acting bronchodilator.

Assessment: 68‑year‑old male with COPD on optimal inhaled therapy and recent 5‑day course of prednisone. New spiculated 2.3 cm nodule in LLL on contrast‑enhanced CT. Differential includes primary lung cancer (especially squamous cell carcinoma or metastatic disease), granulomatous disease (post‑TB cavity or sarcoidosis), infectious etiologies (abscess, invasive fungal), and benign post‑bronchogenic processes.

Plan:
1. Add tiotropium 18 µg once daily for COPD optimization.
2. Continue inhaled corticosteroid/long‑acting bronchodilator (fluticasone/salmeterol BID) and albuterol PRN.
3. Send sputum sample for culture and AFB smear (even if scant); consider induced sputum.
4. Discuss possible referral to thoracic surgery or interventional pulmonology for image‑guided needle biopsy or wedge resection based on CT findings.
5. Educate patient on symptom red‑flag (increasing hemoptysis, unexplained weight loss, new dyspnea).
6. Schedule follow‑up in 2 weeks to review CT results, sputum culture, and consider bronchoscopy. If patient deteriorates sooner, advise to call clinic immediately.
7. Continue antihypertensive (lisinopril) and hyperglycemic (metformin) management. Monitor blood pressure and glucose.
8. Reinforce smoking cessation and pulmonary rehab counseling.

Disposition: Patient will return in 2 weeks for CT review and further work‑up. He is instructed to call the clinic for any acute change.
follow_up_recommendations: Return for follow‑up in 2 weeks to review CT and sputum culture results
Arrange possible bronchoscopy or needle biopsy if indicated
Consider referral to thoracic surgery if nodule remains suspicious
Call clinic immediately if hemoptysis increases, dyspnea worsens, or new symptoms arise
Continue smoking cessation education and pulmonary rehab enrollment

ce01efdb.content:
**Chief Complaint**: Discussion of diagnostic biopsy results and treatment planning for newly diagnosed pulmonary malignancy.

**History of Present Illness**: Mr. Whitaker, 68‑year‑old male with a significant pulmonary history (COPD, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, prior TB) presents for review of trans‑bronchial needle aspiration (TBNA) pathology. The specimen yielded poorly differentiated malignant cells, immunohistochemistry positive for p63 and a high Ki‑67 proliferation index. The patient reports persistent cough and nighttime fatigue. He has been on a short course of prednisone 40 mg daily for 5 days after initial hemoptysis and cough; inhaled bronchodilator therapy has been initiated.

**Past Medical History**: COPD with emphysema and chronic bronchitis, asthma, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, prior pulmonary tuberculosis.

**Medications**: Albuterol sulfate 90 µg MDI (PRN), Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 µg Twisthaler BID, Tiotropium 18 µg daily, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Metformin 500 mg BID, Aspirin 81 mg daily.

**Allergies**: NKDA.

**Examination**:
- General: Pale sclera, mild dyspnea at rest.
- Respiratory: Decreased breath sounds bilaterally, wheezes.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm.

**Vitals**:
BP 152/92 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 19 /min, Temp 36.6 °C, SpO₂ 92 % RA.

**Objective Test Results**:
- Cardiopulmonary exercise test: VO₂max 10.5 ml/kg/min, max MET 4.8.
- CT chest (previous): 2.3 cm spiculated nodule in left lower lobe.
- CT guided biopsy pathology: poorly differentiated carcinoma, p63+, high Ki‑67.

**Assessment**:
1. Suspected primary squamous cell carcinoma of the lung (stage I–IIA based on size and limited disease). The histology and high Ki‑67 indicate aggressive tumor biology.
2. COPD exacerbation secondary to tumor‑related airway obstruction.

**Differential Diagnoses**:
- Primary lung carcinoma (squamous cell, large cell).
- Metastatic disease to lung.
- Chronic infectious process (e.g., TB relapse, bacterial pneumonia).

**Plan**:
1. **Treatment**: Proceed with stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) as definitive local treatment given the patient’s comorbidities and exercise limitation (max MET 4.8). Plan SBRT simulation and treatment in 2 weeks.
2. **Supportive Care**:
   - Continue inhaled bronchodilator therapy (Albuterol PRN, Fluticasone/salmeterol BID, Tiotropium daily).
   - Blood pressure and glucose monitoring; continue lisinopril and metformin.
   - Consider a short prednisone taper (5 mg daily for 7 days) before SBRT to reduce risk of radiation pneumonitis.
3. **Monitoring**:
   - Weekly symptom check‑ins, vitals, and oxygen saturation.
   - Baseline CBC, CMP, and coagulation profile before each SBRT session.
   - Repeat CT chest at 3 months post‑SBRT to assess treatment response.
4. **Coordination of Care**:
   - Referral to radiation oncology for SBRT planning.
   - Pulmonology follow‑up after first SBRT session for respiratory status.
   - Dietitians for glycemic control.
   - Schedule a follow‑up oncology visit in 1 month.
5. **Education**:
   - Discussed SBRT procedure, expected side effects (e.g., fatigue, cough, esophageal irritation), importance of medication adherence.
   - Emphasized smoking cessation (patient is a former smoker). 

**Follow‑Up**:
- Radiation oncology simulation in 2 weeks.
- Office oncology follow‑up in 1 month and then every 3 months for the first year.
- Pulmonology reassessment after SBRT to evaluate airway function.
- If disease progression or recurrence by 3 months, re‑evaluate for systemic therapy options.
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule SBRT simulation in 2 weeks
Office oncology follow‑up in 1 month
Pulmonology reassessment post‑SBRT
Repeat CT chest at 3 months post‑SBRT
Weekly symptom and vitals monitoring

ce01efdb.content:
Chief Complaint: SBRT simulation and initiation of definitive treatment for confirmed non‑small cell lung carcinoma.

History of Present Illness:
Mr. Whitaker, a 68‑year‑old male with a history of COPD, asthma, emphysema, chronic bronchitis, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, and prior tuberculosis, presents for the first fraction of a four‑fraction SBRT regimen to treat a 2.3 cm spiculated nodule in the left lower lobe, histologically confirmed as poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (p63+, high Ki‑67). He has been on a short course of prednisone 5 mg daily for 7 days pre‑treatment and continues inhaled bronchodilator therapy (Albuterol, Fluticasone/Salmeterol, Tiotropium). His cough is improving and he reports mild fatigue. He tolerates the first fraction well with low‑grade chest discomfort and moderate fatigue, managed with rest.

Physical Examination:
- Vital signs: BP 149/90 mmHg, HR 87 bpm, RR 20 /min, Temp 36.5 °C, SpO₂ 93 % RA (≥92 % post‑fraction).
- Respiratory: no acute distress, mild bilateral wheezes, no end‑tidal crackles.
- Cardiovascular: no murmurs, normal heart sounds.
- Other: no acute adverse effects noted.

Treatment Administered:
- First SBRT fraction: 10 Gy prescribed to the planning target volume (PTV) with strict gating for diaphragmatic motion.

Assessment:
- Stage IA (T1cN0M0) non‑small cell lung carcinoma, currently being treated with definitive SBRT.
- Comorbid COPD and cardiovascular disease; no acute complications from SBRT observed.

Plan:
1. Complete remaining three SBRT fractions (10 Gy each) on the established schedule (end of current week and beginning of next week).
2. Repeat CBC and CMP prior to each subsequent fraction to monitor for hematologic and renal toxicity.
3. Continue prednisone taper: 5 mg/day for 7 days (currently underway), adjust based on fatigue and chest symptoms.
4. Maintain inhaled bronchodilator regimen (Albuterol PRN, Fluticasone/Salmeterol BID, Tiotropium daily) and antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 10 mg daily).
5. Monitor oxygen saturation after each fraction; if desaturation <92 % or new wheeze, consider bronchodilator rescue and possible hospital evaluation.
6. Counsel patient on signs of early radiation pneumonitis (persistent cough, dyspnea, fever) and instruct prompt reporting.
7. Follow‑up:
   - Oncology office visit 1 month post‑SBRT for clinical assessment.
   - Repeat CT chest at 3 months post‑SBRT to evaluate local control.
   - Pulmonary function test in 6 months to assess long‑term lung function.
   - Cardiology consult if BP remains elevated >140/90 mmHg.

Patient education: Reviewed SBRT schedule, potential acute side effects, importance of adherence to medications and follow‑up. He verbalized understanding and is scheduled for next fractions.

Disposition: Mr. Whitaker is stable and will receive the next SBRT fraction as planned.

Repeat CBC and CMP before each fraction

Return to oncology office in 1 month for clinical review
Repeat CT chest at 3 months post‑SBRT
Consider pulmonary function testing in 6 months

Report any new or worsening cough, dyspnea, or fever immediately

ce01efdb.content:
Chief Complaint: Follow‑up after completion of SBRT for Stage IIA left lower lobe carcinoma.

History of Present Illness: A 68‑year‑old male presents for routine post‑SBRT review. He reports a significant reduction in cough compared with baseline, persistent fatigue, and a 3‑kg weight loss over the past month. No hemoptysis or chest pain is noted. He denies fever, dyspnea escalation or new systemic symptoms.

Past Medical History: COPD (emphysema, chronic bronchitis, asthma), hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, history of tuberculosis, Stage IIA pulmonary adenocarcinoma.

Medications: Albuterol sulfate 90 µg (MDI) BID PRN, Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 µg (Twisthaler) BID, Tiotropium 18 µg daily PO, Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily, Metformin 500 mg BID, Aspirin 81 mg PO daily.

Allergies: NKDA.

Physical Examination:
- Vital Signs: BP 147/88 mmHg, HR 85 bpm, RR 18 bpm, temp 36.7°C, SpO₂ 94% RA.
- General: Appears mildly fatigued, weight 172 lb (↓3 lb).
- HEENT: No abnormalities.
- Chest: Mild inspiratory wheezing, no rales or rhonchi. Breath sounds otherwise clear.
- Abdomen: Non‑tender, soft.
- Extremities: No peripheral edema.

Diagnostics: PET‑CT was performed today and demonstrated a residual 1.8 cm left lower lobe lesion with an SUV of 6.2. No distant metastatic disease or lymphadenopathy noted.

Assessment:
1. Residual primary left lower lobe carcinoma post‑SBRT with avid 18‑FDG uptake.
2. No evidence of regional or distant metastasis (Stage IIB in‑field residual disease).
3. Mild COPD symptoms controlled on maintenance inhalers; stable BMI.
4. Chronic hypertension on lisinopril; controlled.
5. Diabetes under control with metformin.

Plan:
- Initiate first cycle of low‑dose palliative chemotherapy: Carboplatin 200 mg/m² IV on day 1, Paclitaxel 80 mg/m² IV on day 1, repeated every 3 weeks.
- Continue existing inhaled bronchodilator therapy (Albuterol, Fluticasone/Salmeterol, Tiotropium). Advise patient on medication adherence.
- Reinforce nutritional counseling—encourage high‑protein, high‑calorie diet to mitigate weight loss; refer to dietitian if weight continues to decline.
- Educate patient on potential chemo‑induced fatigue; recommend activity pacing.
- Monitor CBC, CMP before each chemotherapy cycle.
- Schedule follow‑up oncology visit in 4 weeks to assess tolerance, toxicity, and response. Repeat PET‑CT in 3 months to evaluate for disease regression.
- Encourage prompt reporting of new or worsening symptoms (dyspnea, hemoptysis, fever). Follow the current COPD management plan and continue aspirin for cardiovascular protection.

Patient expressed reluctance to high‑risk surgical salvage; palliative chemotherapy offers a tolerable compromise.

Disposition: Outpatient.

Signature: Dr. X, M.D.
medications: Albuterol sulfate 90 µg MDI BID PRN
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule first cycle of low‑dose carboplatin/paclitaxel in 2 weeks
Repeat CBC and CMP prior to each cycle
Follow‑up oncology visit in 4 weeks
Repeat PET‑CT at 3 months
Continue current COPD inhaler regimen
Maintain adequate caloric/protein intake
Contact clinic if new hemoptysis, worsening dyspnea, or febrile symptoms occur

ce01efdb.content:
Mr. Whitaker, 68‑year‑old male with Stage IIA squamous cell carcinoma of the left lower lobe, presents today for post‑infusion evaluation of his first cycle of low‑dose carboplatin (AUC 2) and paclitaxel (50 mg/m²). He received the full 30‑minute infusion without acute reaction.

**Subjective:** He reports mild leukopenia noted on his day‑after CBC, mild tingling in his extremities consistent with early peripheral neuropathy, persistent cough (although frequency has decreased), and mild fatigue. No dyspnea exacerbation or new chest pain.

**Objective:** Vitals remain stable: BP 149/90 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, RR 19 /min, temp 36.5°C, SpO₂ 93% on RA. No acute allergic reaction observed during the infusion.

**Assessment:** 1) **Chemotherapy‑induced neutropenia** – WBC 2.3 K/µL; pegfilgrastim (6 mg SC) started to mitigate further drop and reduce febrile neutropenia risk. 2) **Early peripheral neuropathy** likely related to paclitaxel; patient reports mild tingling only, no motor deficit. 3) **COPD/emphysema exacerbation** – persistent dyspnea; inhaler therapy unchanged. 4) **Risk of infection** – neutropenic state; monitor for fever, chills, malaise. 5) **Consideration of radiation pneumonitis** – patient completed SBRT 1.5 weeks prior; no new respiratory symptoms beyond baseline dyspnea.

**Plan:**
- Continue current chemotherapeutic protocol: next cycle of carboplatin (AUC 2) + paclitaxel (50 mg/m²) scheduled in 4 weeks.
- Pegfilgrastim 6 mg SC to be given within 72 hrs of each cycle; CBC will be repeated at day 5 post‑infusion and again 7 days after pegfilgrastim.
- Continue inhaled therapy: albuterol PRN, fluticasone/salmeterol BID, tiotropium 18 µg daily.
- Counsel patient on signs of infection (fever >38 °C, chills, persistent cough) and to seek immediate care.
- Monitor neuropathic symptoms; consider dose adjustment or neuroprotective agent if progressing.
- Encourage rest, adequate hydration, and continue nutritional counseling.
- Plan for repeat PET‑CT in 3 months to assess residual disease.
- Follow‑up oncology visit in 4 weeks (or sooner if symptom changes).
- Maintain routine pulmonology follow‑up; consider pulmonary rehabilitation referral for dyspnea management.
- No changes in antihypertensive or diabetes therapy for now.

**Disposition:** Patient tolerating treatment well, advised to contact clinic if fever or worsening symptoms occur before next scheduled visit.
diagnoses_considered: Bacterial or viral infection due to neutropenia
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule next chemotherapy cycle in 4 weeks
Return for CBC at day 5 post‑infusion and at 7 days after pegfilgrastim
Reassess for signs of infection or worsening neuropathy
Maintain inhaler therapy and rest until next visit
Repeat PET‑CT in 3 months to evaluate residual tumor
Refer for pulmonary rehabilitation if dyspnea persists
Contact clinic immediately if fever >38 °C or new chest pain

ce01efdb.content:
Subjective:
- Patient reports increasing shortness of breath at rest, worsening productive cough, and growing lethargy over the past week.
- Denies chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or hemoptysis at this visit.
- Notes that his overall symptom burden has escalated following completion of SBRT and two cycles of low‑dose carboplatin/paclitaxel.
- Declines additional systemic chemotherapy; prefers symptom‑focused/palliative approach.

Objective:
Vitals:
- BP 158/92 mmHg
- HR 100 bpm
- RR 22/min
- Temp 37.4°C
- SpO₂ 88% on room air

Physical Exam:
- General: appears tired, mildly distressed.
- Chest: dull percussion over left lower lung field; decreased breath sounds without wheezes or crackles; no chest wall tenderness.
- Cardiac: regular rhythm, no murmurs.
- Extremities: no edema.

Labs/Imaging:
- Thoracentesis yielded 350 mL serosanguinous fluid; cytology positive for malignant cells.
- CT chest repeat: left hilar node measuring 1.5 cm, faint para‑aortic uptake; no new distant metastases.
- Prior PET‑CT shows residual left lower lobe lesion (SUV 6.2), no new sites.

Assessment:
- Stage IIIA non‑small cell lung carcinoma with malignant pleural effusion; progressive nodal involvement.
- COPD exacerbation secondary to tumor burden.
- Hypertension mildly elevated; Diabetes controlled.
- Possible early radiation pneumonitis cannot be ruled out.

Plan:
1. Symptom control:
   - Continue current inhaled therapy regimen (Albuterol 90 µg BID PRN, Fluticasone/Salmeterol 250/50 µg BID, Tiotropium 18 µg daily).
   - Begin low‑dose oral prednisone 20 mg daily for 5 days with taper to manage inflammatory response.
   - Initiate supplemental oxygen 2 L/min via nasal cannula to maintain SpO₂ ≥92%; titrate as needed.
2. Management of pleural effusion:
   - Refer to interventional pulmonology for possible therapeutic thoracentesis if symptomatic recurrence.
   - Discuss pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter as palliative options.
3. Oncology:
   - Patient declined further cytotoxic chemo; discuss possible palliative SBRT to residual lesion or nodal radiation.
   - PET‑CT now scheduled in 3 months to reassess metabolic activity; consider earlier imaging if clinical status worsens.
4. Cardiovascular & metabolic:
   - Continue Lisinopril 10 mg daily; consider dose adjustment if BP remains >150/90.
   - Continue Metformin 500 mg BID; monitor renal function due to chemo exposure.
5. Supportive:
   - Encourage smoking cessation, nutritional counseling, and graded exercise plan.
   - Provide education on signs of infection, worsening dyspnea, or pleural effusion recurrence.
6. Follow‑up:
   - Return to clinic in 2 weeks for reassessment of symptoms, vitals, and O₂ requirement.
   - Urgent visit if SpO₂ <90% at rest, new chest pain, or fever >38°C.
   - Palliative care consult within week to coordinate symptom management and end‑of‑life planning.
